name: Avenc de sa Moneda
starting_point: Upper part of the Costa d'en Blanes urbanization
equipment: Multiple descent routes available. Generally: 50 m rope, 2-3 bolt plates, carabiners, slings, 2-4 protectors (varies by route)
description: A spectacular pothole in the Serra de na Burguesa with a massive main chamber exceeding 40x40 m, beautifully decorated with formations, large stalagmitic masses, and impressive columns — including a remarkable 19 m column with plants growing at the top. Natural light from the entrance creates dramatic lighting effects, and multiple descent routes of varying difficulty make it suitable for both sporting and speleological interests.
full_description:
The "Avenc de sa Moneda" is located in the Serra de na Burguesa within the S'Hostalet estate, near the headwaters of a small stream. To approach it, it is necessary to access the uppermost area of the Costa d'en Blanes urbanization (Calviá) where we will find space to leave our vehicle in a small plot. Once past the gate of the S'Hostalet estate, we will walk along a track in continuous descent until reaching a crossroads where we will take a westerly direction for 200 meters until reaching the first left curve, at which point we will leave the path and head off-trail to the right. Within a few minutes we will cross a very small stream bed, and once on the other side we need to look for a larger stream bed that runs parallel to our path. Walking along the slope of the stream, we will have no great difficulty finding its enormous entrance. We must not confuse it with the "Avenc de N'Andreu," which is found about 25 meters before but has a small entrance.

If we decide to descend via the route detailed below, we will descend on a gentle slope for about eight or nine meters until reaching the first and only rebelay, which we will pass without difficulty to "land" on a beautiful ledge after 3 meters of descent that can serve as a waiting area for the rest of the group. The vegetation sheltered by the pothole begins to make its appearance at this spot, where the branches of a fig tree are within our reach; plants also grow on the ledge as well as in the vertical shaft. On a 19-meter-high column already visible from the ledge, small plants also grow — a certainly curious phenomenon. All that remains is a final stretch of 16 meters, most of which is a beautiful aerial descent from which one has a lovely perspective of the cavity ("Main Hall"), before we set down on the highest part of a pile of detritus. The hall in which we find ourselves, of considerable dimensions exceeding 40x40 meters, is of extraordinary beauty as it is carpeted with parietal and ceiling formations, not to mention the large stalagmitic masses and great columns that fill much of the hall and make it recognizable among all others. Of note is the great 19-meter-high column near the entrance and next to the detritus falling from outside, which is striking not only for its vertical perfection but also for the plants growing at the top to take advantage of the exterior light. The natural light from outside plays a very important role in this cave, as depending on its intensity the illumination changes, giving the main hall one appearance or another, which at the same time allows for different photographs of the same spot. We can continue descending on the left side of the detritus via a slippery ramp — especially if wet — until reaching the bottom of the hall, where moving around becomes much more comfortable. Of note is how the formations closest to the entrance of natural light are covered in green algae due to humidity and contact with the exterior; one doesn't want to think what appearance all these formations would have if they were removed from exterior light in an active state. The cavity is practically composed of just this great hall, since the small chambers that can be observed in its surroundings, mainly on the SW side, are divided by stalagmitic flowstones that form the partition. On the SW side, on the other side of the stalagmitic barrier that divides the hall, there is a chamber laden with beautiful and abundant formations. In the place where the cavity appears to end, to the right one can continue visiting the cave on two levels: the upper one ends after a left turn, and the lower one will take us to a small shaft ("Shaft Hall") from which, descending through it, we will emerge into the "Main Hall" below the stalagmitic mass. At the northern end of the great hall, at the bottom of a small shaft of approximately 7 meters, one can access without great difficulty. On the west side, behind some formations and rocks, a small ramp will place us at the top of a shaft of almost 20 meters, which one can climb down with great care, though it is best to rig a rope at its top using a natural anchor to facilitate the task. Five meters from the bottom of the shaft there is a small level, and 5 meters further down another of greater development, which we will access through a narrow hole and which marks the deepest point of the cavity. On the first level, multiple bone remains can be seen, probably belonging to some rodent (pending identification), of which no skull remains were found.

A characteristic of this cavity that differentiates it from many others in the Serra de na Burguesa is that the origin of its formation does not appear to come from a fracture in the terrain; nor does the shaft appear to have developed through dissolution, so everything indicates that it collapsed, falling upon the cavity.

The vegetation found inside this pothole is one of its great attractions, and for our part, lacking botanical knowledge, we will not go into detail until we obtain the necessary information.

Rigging I.

At the lowest part of the pothole entrance and next to the standing skeleton of a tree, there are two spits drilled into the rock that can serve to rig the rope anchor. A small descent of approximately 10 meters will place us on a small ledge of about 3 meters where we will make a rebelay by placing the second plate on a new existing spit. Once on the ledge, all that remains is the final descent, first against the wall to do the last meters aerially. Obvious friction points will require the use of protectors.

Material: 50-meter rope, 2 plates, 2 carabiners, 1 sling, 4 protectors.

Rigging II.

Starting from the same point as the previous route, this route goes almost horizontally toward the place where the fig tree grows, walking very carefully along the wall until reaching a rock where a spit is installed for the first rebelay. From this spot we will again head down a gentle ramp to descend to the lower level, where using a sling we will rig on a natural anchor twice in succession — this creates a traverse line. At the vertex of the ledge, a new rebelay on an existing spit will allow us to descend.

Material: 50-meter rope, 3 plates, 5 carabiners, 2 slings, 2 or 3 protectors.

Rigging III.

This is the most spectacular route. On the north face of the pothole, spits are installed where we will rig the anchor, and as soon as we hang on the wall we will use another to install the plate in a single aerial rebelay that, at 41.5 meters, will deposit us at the bottom of the pothole.

Material: 50-meter rope, 2 plates and two carabiners.

At least one other way of descending comes to mind, starting from the highest part of the entrance, on the west side.

In summary, a beautiful pothole suitable for all tastes, as it satisfies both the strictly sporting and the speleological aspects. If the visit to the "Avenc de sa Moneda" does not fill a full day of caving, it could be completed by visiting the "Avenc de N'Andreu," which is found just 25 meters away and which we will probably have stumbled upon. It is not of great interest.
